title: Data Leakage pada Data Spasial dan Spatial Cross-Validation
key_concepts: Spatial data is highly prone to target leakage because neighboring points share information (spatial autocorrelation).
Standard random splitting (k-fold) is dangerous for spatial data. A point in the test set might be physically right next to a point in the training set, artificially inflating validation performance.
Spatial cross-validation fixes this by splitting data geographically. A model is trained on one region and tested on a separate, distant region to ensure true generalization.
This is the application of 'praktik pipeline yang benar' specific to spatial data.

definitions.meaning: Metode validasi di mana pemisahan data latih dan uji dilakukan berdasarkan batas wilayah geografis, bukan secara acak.
Disiplin dalam menghindari kebocoran data, yang dalam konteks spasial berarti memblokir observasi yang berdekatan agar tidak terpisah ke partisi latih dan uji.

examples: Predicting deforestation: if pixels are split randomly, the test pixels are surrounded by train pixels. Spatial block splitting forces the model to predict on an entirely unseen forest tract.

figures.caption: Pemisahan acak vs. pemisahan blok spasial (spatial cross-validation).
figures.description: Dua peta berdampingan. Kiri: titik latih dan uji tersebar acak bak garam dan merica. Kanan: peta dibagi menjadi blok-blok besar, di mana seluruh blok A menjadi latih dan seluruh blok B menjadi uji.

exclude: Overly mathematical definitions of block buffering. Keep it intuitive.
relations: Crucial tie-back to Chapter 2 (leakage). Spatial autocorrelation from s02 is the *reason* s03 leakage happens.
